docs.title: My Workflows
docs.text: This is one page: each workflow has a short default view, then an expand control that reveals the full hyperlinked narrative (Google Docs, GitHub placeholders, and public sources). Use the table of contents (right) or the headings below to jump. References for each workflow remain separate pages in the nav.

docs.title: Report Generation
docs.text:
When I first joined this company, it was because I wanted to be helpful to the entire process of constructing a neuromodulation device and leverage my unique expertise in psychology, neuroimaging and research to be an effective developer of this device's features.

What we figured was the most immediate fit was the fact that the EEG part of the trifecta (tDCS+CET+EEG) is most underdeveloped, and having worked in an EEG lab that was the most critical question, and as Saakshi asked me in the very first meeting; given the raw EEG data we obtain, how best to get something useful out of it?

Through a series of events, we generated many different reports, using both my manual editing and coding repos, from the first hand-typed report containing hemispherical symmetry information and relative PSD value graphs generated by the code (as presented by Dr Bannerjee in ANCIPS), to the final version live right now, which uses two scripts, core_processing_pipeline.py and json_export.py, with the latter calling functions from the former to generate a json file with pre-post comparison metrics, that can be converted by Ashish's code to fill out the pdf we generate on doctor dashboard, while core_processing_pipeline.py is capable of generating detailed metrics for a single EEG session.

Then, we made an HTML document to infer it in detail, usable for sales, patients and doctors;

Finally set up a repo to use json notation, css styling and react for rendering and saving pdfs, that can now generate versatile documents with appropriate sources for design and content!

Expand: full narrative and sources

Canonical numbered list: References.

docs.title: Reporting and PDF generation
docs.text:
When Saakshi and I first met, the question posed was “how to get something useful” out of our EEG data, given the fact that

  • a) we do not know anything about its accuracy and
  • b) its constraints (4 channel data, in a csv format, at 250/500 hz, recorded with eyes open/closed at rest)

The first step was to set up a processing pipeline that could measure frequency power (most appropriate for time series data such as ours).

For a from-scratch problem (unknown hardware properties, few channel info, unique format), we had to build a from-scratch pipeline.

Existing pipelines such as HAPPE, EEGLab in Matlab, and MNE in Python etc. depended on assumptions about signal type and electrodes that did not apply to us, so using different libraries we constructed a sequence more suited for our data (1).

This was posed with an assignment from Lakshay and Swapnil, where known generated waveforms were given in the same 4-channel CSV format and I was told to find out the composite waves (2). I did this with SciPy’s FFT library (3).

However, a direct Fourier transform does not help our use case, since high contact impedance, slow drift, physiological artefacts and electronic artefacts contaminate all the frequency bands and have to be removed.

See waveform basics (4) for a full analysis of our use case, including scope and limitations of conventional EEG libraries.

So the first clinical report was made (5), which focused on generating PSD values in a graph form, and also hemispherical symmetry of the detected wave frequencies and amplitudes. See references for building normative ranges for frontal presence of the frequency bands at Normative EEG Database (6).

It then made certain inferences about symptom risks and cognitive indicators (for example, excessive theta+delta activity corresponding to poor cognitive control and frontal alpha asymmetry as a marker for withdrawal/avoidance).

This was made into a report that was consequently presented at ANCIPS by Debanjan Bannerjee.

Many reports were then handmade, as we found doctors like the feature and the delight factor. With every report, we refined the structure of reporting as well as the content, generating different visualizations and descriptions, which were iteratively refreshed and new ones brought in to replace the older ones.

Soon after, Lakshay requested an automated pipeline (7) that could do end-to-end processing plus report generation from a single CSV, to be presented to investors in Bangalore. This was done in collaboration with Kiwimesh Illiyas and Atul, and we automated the process such that within a little more than 10 seconds the CSV could be processed and a report could be generated for the doctor dashboard.

This moved us onto ANCIPS, for which I decided to redesign the pipeline based on what I had learnt, namely:

  1. The maximum contamination existed in the delta band and higher gamma bands, consistent with research on the use of few, frontal and dry electrodes.
  2. Log-power alpha symmetry, specific ratios of relative PSD values, were far more reliable for our use case than individual power bands as a marker.
  3. The relative deviation of an individual’s cognitive parameters over a period of time (score accumulates as there is more deviation and more number of epochs affected) could be a source of conditional logic for what high/low values of each cognitive parameter could indicate.

See EEG Basics → Cognitive Parameters (8) for rationale of calculating reliable markers, and EEG Validation → frontal PSD (9) for frontal-specific functions of frequency power bands. Additionally, see Cognitive Parameters (10) and Conditional Logic (11) for code implementation of the same.

This is what we presented at ANCIPS, along with a “multi-session” report that could show the difference in EEG markers between spaced sessions, which was my in-detail PDF-generating script focused on EEG processing (12, 13).

As of now, what exists as the production-current software are core_processing_pipeline.py, which has functions for loading the CSV data, calculating impedance, high-pass, low-pass and notch filtering, conducting artefact (eye blink and muscle movement) rejection using bipolar correlation between FP1 and FP2 based on MNE, and then finally Welch PSD.

That leads to cognitive parameters and their comparison generated by json_export.py, which calls these functions then outputs JSON lists which are used by Ashish to display in the report (1).

The next concern was understandability, for patients, doctors and salespeople, such that they comprehend the reporting content and structure. Therefore ease-eeg-manual was made to launch a cohesive story about what is EEG, the power bands, the cognitive parameters, all the report sections with elaboration on what is the content, how to infer it, etc.

The HTML document was not in PDF format. An entire repo was set up to use JSON text (actual content), TypeScript for defining how JSON will be presented on React (mapping functions), CSS for styling (colour, page margins, etc.) and finally Vite dev, which mounts the JSON on a local server, launches the app to render and save the PDF. Now, using appropriate inputs for the content, document style and design, Cursor/Claude can make any PDF using this repo (14).

docs.title: Patents
docs.text:
A detailed analysis of Indian, international and foreign patent laws helped us define key concepts, of which 'Freedom To Operate' became a central theme: we understood broad claims of methods and systems (such as those by Flow, Soterix) to be formed to restrict others from making and selling similar devices, while narrower claims of devices and applications (such as those listed by Swapnil, Boston Engineering) to strengthen one's own niche and prevent prosecution from broader patents as defined before. Further clarity on how Freedom to Operate works out practically was demonstrated with the case study on Oura, with a special emphasis of their prosecution of Ultrahuman, which taught us the importance of both a) designing around existing prior art and b) how a patent can be used to prosecute similar products.

A two-pronged approach to writing patents was identified, a top-down approach aimed at writing a broad patent that claims a novel system of neuromodulation, and specific continuation and/or parallel patents which change one aspect of our system (monitoring, interactive display/therapeutic techniques, stimulation) and write a continuation patent. Close patents are analyzed for the scope and the limitations of their claims, as well as IPC indices for the same.

The substantive, novel step synthesized from this analysis was that the prior art of a closed-loop system of neuromodulation has been patented, however, the technique to personalize it, using data that is outside the system is not considered. Instead of detect state-> trigger modulation, we specify logic of decision making (control policy) that considers patient characteristics, subjective information and other inputs to decide therapy as a whole, not just stimulation.

Expand: full narrative and sources

Expanded narrative. The canonical numbered and public URL list is References. Below is the mediating overview between the short executive narrative and raw research notes, with inline links. Replace YOUR_DOC_ID everywhere you paste real Google Doc IDs.

Deep-dive material for teammates with access lives in a Google Doc structured by section: hub, patent basics primer, FTO, Oura vs Ultrahuman, closest patents, and claims → limitations → design-around. Public sources used while building this understanding include Wikipedia, WIPO, USITC notices, and patent PDFs linked from References.

docs.title: Executive framing
docs.text:
Reviewing Indian, international, and foreign patent practice helped the team align on a few recurring ideas. Freedom To Operate (FTO) became a central theme: competitors often pursue broad method and system claims (for example in the neuromodulation space, filings associated with names such as Flow and Soterix) that can make it harder for others to commercialize similar devices without careful claim analysis. Narrower filings around specific devices and applications (including work attributed to colleagues such as Swapnil and organizations such as Boston Scientific in our notes) can strengthen a niche and interact differently with those broader fences.

FTO is not only “one patent at the system level.” It requires mapping subsystems and components—including parts you might buy from vendors—because infringement and design-around questions can attach to partial feature overlap. See the FTO notes in Google Docs and the WIPO background on FTO-style analysis.

Practical clarity came from following consumer hardware litigation as a teaching example: Oura’s actions against Ultrahuman (and others) illustrated how broad claims plus doctrine-of-equivalents style reasoning can support enforcement, and why incremental hardware or app tweaks may not establish a non-obvious distinction if the claim language still reads on the accused product. Follow the Oura case study notes, USITC notice (PDF), related USITC notice, and representative patent PDFs (US11868178, US12222759, US12346159 B2, US12353244 B2). Settlements (for example with RingConn) show how commercial resolutions carve out product surfaces (apps, accessories) even when patents remain contentious.

For our strategy, a two-pronged approach emerged:

  1. Top-down: a broad filing that states a novel neuromodulation system (claims aligned to what we actually build and can support with embodiments).
  2. Continuations / parallel filings: change one controlled axis at a time—monitoring, interactive display / therapeutic techniques, stimulation—so each application has a clear delta from the parent disclosure.

Parallel work includes landscaping close patents: scope and limitations of their claims, IPC classes, and explicit design-around notes. The table of “relevant patents” in our research (see closest-patent analysis in Google Docs and landscape examples such as US9339642, US12453854, US9511222, artifact-detection example PDF) contrasts core idea, claim breadth, limitations, and where our architecture differs (for example control-policy emphasis vs pure state → trigger stimulation loops).

Substantive novelty (synthesis): prior art often describes closed-loop neuromodulation as detect state → trigger modulation. Our direction emphasizes personalization using data that can live outside the immediate biosignal loop—patient characteristics, subjective inputs, and other context—so that therapy is chosen by an explicit control policy (decision logic), not only by a short-latency state classifier feeding stimulation. That distinction is the bridge from landscape analysis to claim drafting and FTO; see claims and design-around notes.

docs.title: Patent basics primer
docs.text:
Before FTO and landscaping work, the notes anchored on a small set of main questions: Is the device patentable? Are we infringing anyone in a given jurisdiction? How do we file so the claims match what we can disclose and defend? How do we search top-down (by assignee / competitor) vs bottom-up (by keywords and IPC)? Full notes: Google Doc — patent basics primer.

Core vocabulary included specifications (sufficiency of disclosure), independent vs dependent claims, the International Patent Classification (IPC) system, and the doctrine of equivalents (function–way–result; all-elements style tests in US jurisprudence; see also Cornell WEX). Patent families—continuations, continuation-in-part, divisionals—explain why similar-looking documents differ in claim scope while sharing priority. Background: patent (overview), patent claim, prior art, certificate of contested validity.

docs.title: Freedom To Operate (FTO)
docs.text: FTO analysis asks whether a product feature-set is covered by any enforceable claim in relevant jurisdictions and periods. A recurring mistake is only searching whole-system patents. Subsystems (algorithms, UI flows, electrode control modes) need their own mapping because partial overlap can still matter for components and software. Working FTO notes (Google Doc) · WIPO PDF context

docs.title: Oura vs Ultrahuman (case study)
docs.text:
The research narrative used Oura’s enforcement as a case study: complaints to the USITC (Section 337), arguments that competitor hardware and software features still fall within broad claim language, parallel litigation threads (including India), and settlements that exempt certain product areas. Case study notes (Google Doc) · USITC notice · USITC notice (related) · patent PDFs on References (items 11–14)

This is not legal advice; it is a pattern language for how claim breadth and equivalence interact with product differentiation.

docs.title: Analysis of closest patents
docs.text:
Landscape work listed assignees and product areas in non-invasive neuromodulation and related monitoring, then focused on a matrix of patents: core idea, claim scope, limitations, and our design-around angle. Closest-patent notes (Google Doc) · US9339642 · US12453854

Examples captured in the notes include:

Patent (examples) Core idea (summary) Typical limitation called out in notes Design-around direction (examples) US9339642 Multi-electrode stimulation + integrity / contact sensing Hardware + sensing focus; not full adaptive-task coupling Differentiate on control / software policy and task coupling US12453854 Biosignal stream → signature → modulate stimulation Strong reliance on state/signature mapping Emphasize policy and outcomes beyond a single signature label US2015351655 Implant-leaning closed loop: condition → stimulate Event / condition triggers Continuous optimization framing vs binary triggers US10932688 EEG + behavior + adaptation Anchors to neural targets Outcome-driven control vs fixed neural targets US20190069796 EEG-informed learning tasks Weaker stimulation–task coupling Explicit coupled task–stimulation law US20230191130 RL for stimulation Stimulation-only optimization Joint task + stimulation co-optimization NeuroPace-style / seizure Event detect → stimulate Binary event loop Avoid pure event binary; use continuous performance framing

IPC codes collected for neuromodulation / EEG-related buckets appear in the raw notes (A61N, A61B families) for search strategy.

docs.title: Claims → limitations → design-around strategy
docs.text:
Claims set the legal boundary; limitations narrow or sharpen that boundary (preamble, transition phrases like comprising, element-by-element mapping). Design-around means changing the product or claim strategy so that each element of a relevant claim is not met—or so that your novel combination is non-obvious and distinctly supported in the specification. Readings: patent claim, doctrine of equivalents.

The synthesis section above is the intended inventive step story: move from “closed loop exists in prior art” to “how therapy is personalized and decided using richer inputs and an explicit control policy,” rather than only detect state → stimulate. Drafting / strategy notes (Google Doc)

docs.title: Clinical Success
docs.text:
To drive the publications, research collaborations and establish Marbles Health as a subject matter expert in the domain of neuromodulation, we have undertaken many projects. my contributions towards this started with researching the use of tDCS in stroke for the AIIMS NCAHT proposal, which yielded useful links for reviewing the use of tDCS for different neurological disorders, the setting of optimal parameters for motor functioning and ways to verify it via imaging and assessments. This was linked to research for Dr.s Shabbari and Sridhar, extending the use of tDCS especially C3/C4 for motor rehabilitation. This also led to the conception of License Requirements and a draft mail for Sridhar promoting the use of EASE by Marbles as a domestic, readily-available device as a better option than internationally sourced systems, the arguments contained within can be extrapolated to argue for the use of our device by other Indian academic institutions and hospitals.

We submitted an abstract for NIMHANS which posited that ERPs present frontally in some of our tasks (Go/No-Go and Stroop) can be reliably detected by our headband, which was selected by the OC but we abandoned its pursuit, but serves as a good template for future research.

I also documented more than 50-60 research papers for our systematic review, additionally selecting a risk of bias tool and then scoring all applicable research papers accordingly, and finally writing a table and summary of Neural Studies for the Results section.

Currently, the research proposal that I have written for Safdarjung is under review, and documents our first default+site implementation, to be followed by personalization as a factor in research.

I further documented patient cases, with their assessment and session data extracted into studies for our monthly newsletter;

And also wrote an article on how brain stimulation is emerging as a bridge to address health gaps, present both globally and in the domestic context.

Further content included a webinar on the use of tDCS over or parallely with SSRIs/SNRIs,

and 3 presentations for Dr Kavitha and her associates to be presented in ANCIPS.

Further, I documented research on how current spreads over different sources in tdcs/eeg electrodes, which will serve as a basis for different design implementations of future EASE devices.

Future directions: DSIR recognition; new paradigm for CET tasks.

Expand: full narrative and sources

Expanded narrative. Canonical links: References (Google Docs + clinical_references.txt placeholder).

docs.title: Publications, proposals, and positioning
docs.text: To drive publications, research collaborations, and to establish Marbles Health as a subject matter expert in neuromodulation, we have undertaken many projects. My contributions started with researching the use of tDCS in stroke for the AIIMS NCAHT proposal — surfacing literature on tDCS across neurological disorders, motor parameters, and verification via imaging and assessments. That connected to work for Drs. Shabbari and Sridhar on C3/C4 motor rehabilitation (clinical hub Doc). It also led to license requirements and a draft mail for Sridhar promoting EASE as a domestic, readily available device versus imported systems — arguments that extend to other Indian academic institutions and hospitals.

docs.title: NIMHANS abstract
docs.text: We submitted an abstract for NIMHANS arguing that frontal ERPs in Go/No-Go and Stroop could be read with our headband; it was selected by the OC but we did not pursue it further — it remains a template for future research. Link supporting notes in your clinical Google Doc.

docs.title: Systematic review
docs.text: I documented more than 50–60 research papers, selected a risk-of-bias tool, scored applicable papers, and wrote the Neural Studies tables and summary for the Results section. Tie-out: systematic review / RoB section and the exported reference list (clinical_references.txt on GitHub).

docs.title: Safdarjung proposal
docs.text: The Safdarjung research proposal (under review) documents our first default EEG + tDCS + CET protocol and at-home vs clinic (“site”) implementation, with personalization reserved for later studies. Safdarjung protocol notes.

docs.title: Newsletters, articles, webinars, ANCIPS
docs.text: I packaged patient cases (assessments and session data) for the monthly newsletter and wrote on brain stimulation bridging health gaps globally and in India. We ran a webinar on tDCS with SSRIs/SNRIs and prepared three ANCIPS presentations for Dr Kavitha and colleagues.

docs.title: Hardware-oriented research
docs.text: I documented how current spreads across tDCS/EEG electrodes — a basis for future EASE device designs.

docs.title: Future directions
docs.text: DSIR in-house R&D recognition; new CET task paradigm — track in your clinical hub Doc when milestones exist.

docs.title: Signal Quality
docs.text:
In an effort to test the relative signal quality from 220k ohm resistor EEG compared to the 47k ohm one, Swapnil Lakshay and I concluded the best way to experimentally validate digital signaling decisions would be to first see how the functions work on known, ideal data recorded with our EEG electrodes.

This gave a new opportunity to rebuild our pipeline from scratch in a novel way, i.e. calibrating the dsp modules to known hardware properties in a sequence of steps that additively boost signal-to-noise ratio and reliability.

The first completed part of this exists within the signal quality repo, which has modules for designing different filters (types and parameters), FFT calculations, visualization, calibration and testing scripts. Optimization scripts can test different combinations of filter types, parameters and sequences of steps, and compare results to known test data (included under hardware lab) to select the modules which work best for us. After running experimental and test scripts, final decisions were compiled in detect_sine.py which has been verified to correctly identify the frequency/amplitude of generated ideal waves and reconstruct them in the time domain.

This will be followed by ERP experimentations (where averaged trials show known peaks) and artefact generation protocols to continue refining our dsp modules and reach state-of-the-art reliability, which this documentation will help to construct into a narrative for publishable results in a scientific journal.

Expand: full narrative and sources

Expanded narrative. Canonical links: References (repo paths, project docs, optional Google Docs).

docs.title: Motivation: ideal data first
docs.text: With Swapnil, Lakshay, and the team, we asked how to validate digital signalling choices for EEG when comparing hardware contexts (e.g. 220 kΩ vs 47 kΩ resistor paths). The conclusion was to run the processing stack on sinusoidal tones at known frequency and Vpp, recorded through the same EEG electrodes used in hardware work — so each stage is judged against ground truth before clinical noise dominates interpretation.

docs.title: Rebuild: DSP aligned to hardware
docs.text: That enabled a ground-up rebuild: calibrate DSP modules to known hardware properties in a sequence of steps that additively improve SNR and reliability. Document the calibration story in SIGNAL_PROCESSING_NARRATIVE.md and the repo layout in PROJECT_MAP.md once the repo is public.

docs.title: What exists in-repo
docs.text:
The Signal Quality repository (root) includes modules for filter design (types and parameters), FFT, visualization, calibration, and testing. Optimization scripts explore filter types, parameters, and step order, comparing outputs to reference test data (including hardware lab captures). After experiments, decisions were consolidated in detect_sine.py, verified to recover frequency and amplitude of ideal waves and reconstruct them in the time domain.

Optional working notes: filter optimization experiments; lab exports (Signal Quality.txt, Filter Optimization Experiment.txt) — add blob links in References when committed.

docs.title: Next: ERP and artefacts
docs.text: ERP experiments (averaged trials with known peaks) and artefact protocols are next — to refine DSP toward state-of-the-art reliability and a journal-ready narrative.

docs.title: Patents — research notes
docs.text:
Long-form research for the Patents workflow was developed in Patent Basics.txt (working title) and extended in Google Docs.

  • The mediating overview on this site is the recommended entry point: Patents overview.
  • You may commit a redacted export of raw notes into this folder (for example patent-basics-notes.md) if you want version history next to the handbook, or keep raw notes only in Google Drive.

Do not commit confidential legal advice or unreleased filing content without review.

docs.title: Closest-patent table (key US references in overview)
docs.text:
These are the same identifiers summarized in the overview; full claim text lives on Justia, USPTO, or Google Patents.

  • US9339642 — multi-electrode stimulation with monitoring / sentinel-style sensing.
  • US12453854 — neurological signature-based adaptive stimulation during tasks.
  • US2015351655 A1 — closed-loop implant style (detect condition → stimulate).
  • US10932688 B2 — EEG + behavior-driven adaptation + stimulation.
  • US20190069796 A1 — EEG-informed learning with adaptive tasks.
  • US20230191130 A1 — reinforcement learning for stimulation optimization.
  • NeuroPace / seizure patents (e.g. US6597954) — event-triggered closed-loop stimulation.
